data.title: All Blacks fire coach Robertson less than two years before World Cup after scathing review
data.description:
New Zealand fired coach Scott Robertson on Thursday less than two years before the Rugby World Cup, after a scathing 2025 performance review and rumours of a player revolt.
The 51-year-old led the team to 20 wins in 27 matches, below powerhouse New Zealand’s expectations, while showing little improvement during his two years in charge.
New Zealand Rugby’s internal review of performance found strong criticism from senior All Blacks, including a suggestion that star player Ardie Savea could walk out.
New Zealand Rugby chair David Kirk said it was the right time to make a change, with the 2027 Rugby World Cup the “key goal”.
“The midpoint in the Rugby World Cup cycle is the right time to look at the All Blacks’ progress over the first two seasons,” Kirk said.
“We’ve taken an extensive look at the team’s progress on and off the field and have subsequently had discussions with Scott on the way forward.

“Both NZR and Scott agree it is in the best interests of the team that he depart his role as head coach.
“On behalf of New Zealand Rugby, I would like to thank Scott for his contribution to the All Blacks.
“As always, he has continued to put the All Blacks first and we respect that he has done the hard but right thing in agreeing to depart.”
Robertson said in a statement he was “gutted” to be leaving the team, but he was “incredibly proud” of what they had achieved.
“Coaching the All Blacks has been the honour of my life,” he said.
“As you can imagine, I am gutted by this outcome. I care deeply about this team.”
Robertson said he had ushered through a talented group of young players, improving depth and setting good foundations for the future.
But he said he agreed to leave after reflecting on the end-of-year review.
Robertson took over as coach in January 2024 with a contract through to the end of the 2027 World Cup in Australia.
His appointment was greeted with much fanfare after he led the Canterbury Crusaders to seven consecutive Super Rugby titles with a reputation as a forward-thinking leader who had strong support from his players.
He endeared himself to fans with his winning record and his habit of breakdancing on the pitch after his team’s title wins.
But under his leadership, the All Blacks have been in a spin.

Robertson lost two of his assistant coaches in his first two years, with Leon MacDonald and Jason Holland quitting, citing differences with the head coach.
Robertson claimed last year that his authority had been undermined, saying that his assistant, Scott Hansen, “effectively operates in the role most would recognise as the head coach”.
But Chris Lendrum, New Zealand Rugby’s professional rugby and performance general manager, said the comments were taken “out of context”.
Otago Highlanders coach Jamie Joseph, the former Japan coach, or former Australia coach Dave Rennie could be potential successors to oversee a packed Test calendar in 2026, in the run-up to the World Cup.
The All Blacks travel to South Africa for an eight-match tour in August and September, playing the Springboks four times.
They also host France, Italy and Ireland in the Nations Cup in July, then play Australia home and away in the Bledisloe Cup before travelling to Europe in November for more Nations Cup matches.

data.title: China’s C919 airliner put through paces by test pilots from European aviation regulator
data.description:
European aviation evaluators have begun test flights of China’s home-grown C919 airliner in Shanghai, the Post has learned, as the manufacturer of the narrowbody jet presses ahead with its bid to secure Western safety certification – a crucial step in the journey to global adoption and being able to compete with industry leaders Boeing and Airbus.
Two test pilots from the European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) have conducted verification flights, according to a source with knowledge of the arrangements. Meanwhile, senior foreign pilots working in China have been deployed to help demonstrate the C919’s reliability.
The moves, following a delay in the high-stakes certification process last year, were part of ongoing exchanges between Commercial Aircraft Corporation of China (Comac) – the C919’s manufacturer – and the European regulator, the source said.
EASA pilots conducted in-flight tests in November on a C919 that took off from Shanghai’s Pudong Airport, near the aircraft’s production base, the source added.
“[EASA] agreed the aircraft is good and safe … other than some teething problems that required tweaks,” the source said.
Another source confirmed the test flights and said there had been an increase in exchanges between Comac and EASA recently.
Comac and EASA have not responded to requests for comment from the Post.
Jason Li Hanming, an aviation and logistics analyst, said progress was being made and EASA’s test flights would be crucial.
“They will focus on how the aircraft can handle extreme situations for real-time, airborne assessment,” he said. “It is arguably the most visible part of the certification, signalling the efforts by both Comac and EASA.”
Flight tests are a key part of compliance demonstration, the third stage of EASA’s four-part assessment regime. They validate critical aspects as the aircraft is put through extreme manoeuvres, stalls and weather environments. After successful flight testing and other demonstrations, EASA reviews the complete compliance package and ensures any issues are resolved before issuing certification.


The C919, China’s challenger to the Boeing 737 and Airbus A320 mainline narrowbody airliners, still needs Western certification to bolster its international standing and fly beyond Chinese skies. That is despite more than two years of smooth domestic operations since May 2023, with four million passengers transported by the end of last year.
In May last year, EASA executive director Florian Guillermet said the approval timeline for the C919 could be extended to “three to six years”, dashing hopes the Chinese airliner would pass inspection soon.
“The C919 cannot be certified in 2025 … We should be certifying within three to six years,” Guillermet said, while adding he was optimistic the plane would eventually receive certification.
“Comac is putting a lot of resources, determination and technical means into this certification,” he said. “I have no doubt they will succeed.”
In recent months, Comac has also tapped Western pilots employed by Chinese airlines to operate C919 flight simulators and participate in technical dialogue with EASA.
“It’s just started as the first check for the CRM (crew resource management) assessment,” the first source said. “The first check was passed, with EASA satisfied about the results, but there will be more.”
CRM refers to a set of procedures, principles and skills for the use of all available resources – crew members, hardware and information – to ensure safety and reduce human error. EASA mandates CRM as part of evaluations of human factor considerations and cockpit design for aircraft type certification.
The first source said that some software issues related to interactions between the crew and the aircraft had been identified in recent inspections, but rectification would only require “small fixes” and no hardware problems were involved.
Li said the mobilisation of Western pilots was part of the manufacturer’s efforts to recruit a range of aviation professionals to make a stronger case for the C919.
“Those foreign captains are elite, with a wealth of experience, and their participation can enhance Comac’s exchanges with EASA and offer valuable input,” he said.

data.title: Unexpected Family movie review: Jackie Chan trades kicks for tears in sentimental drama
data.description:
3/5 stars
Jackie Chan strives to paint himself as a respectable father figure yet again in his new comedy drama Unexpected Family, playing an elderly man struggling with Alzheimer’s disease who mistakes a young loner for his own estranged son.
Short on action but long on ham-fisted life lessons, the debut feature from writer-director Li Taiyan – credited simply as “Tai” – seems tailor-made for the Chinese holiday season, with its fast-paced blend of slapstick humour, domestic squabbles and climactic melodrama.
Ren Jiqing (Chan) is a former athlete with fading faculties who lives a quiet life in Wuhan, China. He is surrounded by a motley group of sympathetic neighbours, including Aunt Jin (Li Ping), Xiaoyue (Karlina Zhang Jianing) and reformed gangster-turned-mechanic Little Jia (Pan Binlong).


When scruffy out-of-towner Bufan (Peng Yuchang) appears on Jia’s doorstep looking for a job, he is offered lodgings in Ren’s storeroom, only for the confused senior to mistake him for his own son, Zhuangzhuang.
Zhuangzhuang was a competitive weightlifter who, 30 years earlier, failed to secure a world championship. It is an episode that still haunts Ren’s fragmented memory.
Overjoyed that his “son” has apparently returned after more than a decade away, Ren takes Bufan in. When Bufan discovers there is an inheritance waiting for him, the neighbours agree to play along with the ruse.
In the film’s first half, Ren’s condition is treated as little more than bumbling forgetfulness and used as a catalyst for a string of comedic high jinks.

Later on, Tai and his cast appear to have been reminded that Alzheimer’s is no laughing matter, and as Ren’s health worsens, the film takes a dramatic – if not unforeseen – turn towards the tragic.
To his credit, Chan is surprisingly convincing as Ren – despite the script offering little understanding of how to actually care for someone with Alzheimer’s and the actor being hampered by some distracting de-ageing make-up during numerous flashbacks.
The seasoned action star steps up admirably, bringing genuine emotion to a climax that forces the father to reconsider how he handled the public disgrace of his famous son.
The themes in Unexpected Family might be delivered with all the subtlety of a 200lb barbell, but they are well intentioned and hard to contest. Families need not be bonded by blood, but are indispensable support structures to be nurtured rather than exploited.

If Chan’s cinematic output over the last decade is any indication, he is eager to demonstrate that he has finally got the message. Based on his performance in Unexpected Family, this reviewer is inclined to agree with him.
Want more articles like this? Follow SCMP Film on Facebook

data.title: Hong Kong stocks extend gains for a fifth day, marking their best run in a year
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks rose for a fifth day on Thursday, as investors bet that gains in Chinese assets would outpace those in the US due to cheaper valuations and ongoing geopolitical uncertainty.
The Hang Seng Index rose 0.6 per cent to 27,169.80 as of 10am local time, extending a 0.6 per cent gain from the previous session. The benchmark’s fifth consecutive day of increase marked its longest winning streak in a year. The Hang Seng Tech Index fell 0.4 per cent.
On the mainland, the CSI 300 Index fell 0.4 per cent, while the Shanghai Composite Index dropped 0.3 per cent.
Electric-car maker BYD advanced 2.3 per cent to HK$99.80, while logistics firm ZTO Express jumped 4 per cent to HK$178.80. Pharmaceutical firm WuXi AppTec advanced 1.6 per cent to HK$120.90 and developer Sun Hung Kai Properties rose 1.9 per cent to HK$110.80. E-commerce giant Alibaba Group Holding added 0.4 per cent to HK$169.60.
Limiting gains, online travel-booking agency Trip.com slumped 18 per cent to HK$465.80 after China launched an antitrust investigation into the company. Online-game provider NetEase slid 1 per cent to HK$218.80.
Asian shares have outperformed Wall Street this year, supported by relatively cheaper valuations and global investors’ diversification away from US assets amid rising geopolitical risks. The Hang Seng Index has climbed 5.3 per cent, outpacing a 1.7 per cent gain in the US benchmark S&P 500.
Elsewhere in Asia-Pacific, Japan’s Nikkei 225 fell 1.5 per cent, while South Korea’s Kospi rose 0.4 per cent and Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 added 0.3 per cent.

data.title: BTS fans in Malaysia ecstatic over comeback concerts but fear scalpers
data.description:
The announcement that BTS will return to Malaysia after more than a decade has sent K-pop fans into a frenzy, with many calling it a once-in-a-lifetime moment.
The South Korean supergroup will stage two concerts in Kuala Lumpur on December 12 and 13, marking its first appearance in the country since 2015, when it performed its Red Bullet Tour at Mega Star Arena.
This will also be BTS’ first-ever concert in Kuala Lumpur as its previous Malaysia performance was held in Kota Kinabalu, Sabah.
For diehard fan Lily Lim, the news was overwhelming.
“I’ve been longing for BTS to come to Kuala Lumpur for five whole years. It’s been 11 years since they last set foot in Malaysia, and they’ve never even performed in Kuala Lumpur before. I’m ecstatic that they’re finally coming to our beautiful city,” she said.
Lim said just one show in Malaysia would not be enough.
“I’m targeting three locations. I’ll try my best to get at least one ticket for Bangkok or Singapore, and one in Malaysia. Seeing them just once will never be enough,” she said.
She hopes to secure VIP tickets in Kuala Lumpur and is already preparing an elaborate strategy to beat the queues, including mobilising friends and family across multiple countries to help secure tickets online.
“Last time, I had more than 70 devices queuing for me, and I still barely got tickets. This time, I’m teaming up with other Armys so we can help each other make our dreams come true,” she said, referring to the band’s fandom.


Like many fans, Lim is worried about scalpers.
“Scalpers make it so much harder for real fans. It’s unfair that people profit off fans’ desperation. Ticketing platforms should tighten their policies and take action against resellers,” she said.
While she has never seen BTS perform as a group, Lim attended solo concerts by members J-Hope and Suga in Singa­pore.
“Those nights were the happiest moments of my life. I can only imagine how magical this reunion concert will be. I know I’ll cry the moment BTS walks on stage and the Army bombs light up the stadium,” she said.
Fans all over social media echoed similar sentiments.
“I really hope I can get a ticket – even if I have to sit by the stairs, I don’t mind. This is truly a wish list moment,” one user said.
“I’m almost a senior citizen already – please give me a ticket first! I’m willing to admit I’m a senior citizen just to get a ticket!” another user said.

As anticipation builds, Malaysian Armys are counting down the days, hopeful that this long-awaited homecoming will finally become a reality.
The concert is part of BTS’ highly anticipated world tour following a three-year hiatus from group activities as all seven members completed their mandatory military service.
The tour, spanning 79 shows across Asia, the United States and Europe, will kick off in Goyang, South Korea, on April 9 and is expected to run until 2027, with additional stops planned for Japan and the Middle East.
This story was first published by The Star

data.title: Musk’s Grok barred from undressing AI images after global backlash
data.description:
Elon Musk’s platform X on Wednesday announced measures to prevent its AI chatbot Grok from undressing images of real people, following global backlash over its generation of sexualised photos of women and children.
The announcement comes after California’s attorney general launched an investigation into Musk’s xAI - the developer of Grok - over the sexually explicit material and multiple countries either blocked access to the chatbot or launched their own probes.
X said it will “geoblock the ability” of all Grok and X users to create images of people in “bikinis, underwear, and similar attire” in those jurisdictions where such actions are deemed illegal.
“We have implemented technological measures to prevent the Grok account from allowing the editing of images of real people in revealing clothing such as bikinis,” X’s safety team said in a statement.
“This restriction applies to all users, including paid subscribers.”
In an “extra layer of protection,” image creation and the ability to edit photos via X’s Grok account was now only available to paid subscribers, the statement added.

The European Commission, which acts as the EU’s digital watchdog, earlier said it had taken note of “additional measures X is taking to ban Grok from generating sexualised images of women and children”.
“We will carefully assess these changes to make sure they effectively protect citizens in the EU,” European Commission spokesperson Thomas Regnier said in a statement, which followed sharp criticism over the non-consensual undressed images.
Global pressure had been building on xAI to rein in Grok after its so-called “Spicy Mode” feature allowed users to create sexualised deepfakes of women and children using simple text prompts such as “put her in a bikini” or “remove her clothes”.
“The avalanche of reports detailing the non-consensual, sexually explicit material that xAI has produced and posted online in recent weeks is shocking,” California Attorney General Rob Bonta said earlier Wednesday.
“We have zero tolerance for the AI-based creation and dissemination of nonconsensual intimate images or of child sexual abuse material.”
Bonta said the California investigation would determine whether xAI violated state law after the explicit imagery was “used to harass people across the internet”.
Indonesia on Saturday became the first country to block access to Grok entirely, with neighbouring Malaysia following on Sunday.
India said Sunday that X had removed thousands of posts and hundreds of user accounts in response to its complaints.
Britain’s Ofcom media regulator said on Monday it was opening a probe into whether X failed to comply with UK law over the sexual images.
And France’s commissioner for children Sarah El Hairy said Tuesday she had referred Grok’s generated images to French prosecutors, the Arcom media regulator and the European Union.
Last week, an analysis of more than 20,000 Grok-generated images by Paris non-profit AI Forensics found that more than half depicted “individuals in minimal attire” - most of them women, and 2 per cent appearing to be minors.

data.title: Hong Kong and Shenzhen forge close ties in finance and AI to build world-class fintech hub
data.description:
Hong Kong and Shenzhen can work closely to empower finance with artificial intelligence, delegates said on Thursday at the China Conference: Greater Bay Area, an event hosted by the South China Morning Post.
Joseph Chan Ho-lim, undersecretary for Financial Services and the Treasury in the Hong Kong government, said Hong Kong and Shenzhen were seeking deeper cooperation between finance and technology.
Chen said at the event in Qianhai, a bonded zone in Shenzhen, that Hong Kong, as a “super-connector” and “super value adder”, could leverage its strength in financial services to support Shenzhen in technology and industrial finance, with the shared ambition of building “a world-class fintech hub”.
“We are also working closely with Qianhai to advance deeper integration between technology and finance across both cities,” Chan said. To do so, Hong Kong would encourage Hong Kong fintech firms to set up subsidiaries and support Shenzhen technology firms to leverage Hong Kong’s capital market.
Xiao Ting, the chairman and executive director at the Bank of Communications (Hong Kong), said AI and finance could empower each other, and the bank had tagged AI as a key area to offer financial support while aligning with Beijing’s AI-plus strategy.
In Hong Kong, the bank’s outstanding loans to tech firms increased by a “three-digit rate” in 2025, and it led a consortium to lend US$100 million to a leading AI company. Xiao did not name the client.
The China conference is designed to bring together leaders and industry experts to discuss opportunities and challenges within the Greater Bay Area, a region covering Hong Kong and Macau as well as Shenzhen and Guangzhou.
Tammy Tam, the publisher of the South China Morning Post, said in her welcome note that the Greater Bay Area also showcased “the best of collaboration”, with each city contributing its unique strengths.
Hong Kong fulfilled its role as the international financial centre and a super-connector between mainland China and the world, and Shenzhen offered technological innovation capacity, including robotics, Tam said.
She added that 2026 would be important for the region thanks to the Asia-Pacific Economic Conference summit later this year.
To navigate a world full of unprecedented challenges and transformations from geopolitical tensions to AI, trusted sources of information and dependable networks would become more critical than ever, she said.

data.title: Trump’s 500% tariff push raises risk of India-US trade freeze
data.description:
US President Donald Trump’s backing for a Senate bill that would allow tariffs of up to 500 per cent on goods from countries buying Russian oil has raised the prospect of a near-freeze in trade between India and its largest export market, as New Delhi continues to absorb steep US duties imposed last year.
Trump last week gave the green light to the proposal, which targets any country that continues to buy Russian oil, petroleum products or uranium, a move seen as part of Washington’s effort to increase pressure on Moscow to reach an agreement to end its war with Ukraine.
The bill would authorise so-called secondary tariffs, penalising third countries that continue to trade in Russian energy rather than sanctioning Russia alone, and would place India among the most exposed economies if enacted.
Nearly half of the US import tariffs imposed earlier were linked to India’s purchases of discounted Russian crude oil, helping push total duties on some Indian goods to 50 per cent.

“Indian exports to the US would no doubt face severe disruption if Trump’s proposed 500 per cent tariff bill is enacted,” Srikumar Menon, a former Indian diplomat, told This Week in Asia.
Such punitive duties would erode India’s export competitiveness across key manufacturing sectors, particularly textiles, auto parts and consumer goods, and would force the country to reorient its trade towards Europe, Asean and Africa, he said.
While the bill has gained Trump’s backing, it has not yet been enacted into law, although voting on the proposal in the US Senate could occur soon.
Should the law be enacted, several of India’s manufacturing exports to the country could be affected, Menon warned, with limited demand for niche sectors such as precision auto components, medical devices, gems and jewellery expected to persist.
India’s Ministry of External Affairs spokesman Randhir Jaiswal said during a media briefing last week that the government was closely following the developments and highlighted the country’s guiding principle for maintaining energy security.
“Our approach takes into account the situation in the global market and the prevailing environment, while also keeping in mind our own imperative of ensuring that our 1.4 billion people are provided energy at affordable prices,” he said.

With discounted Russian oil making up about a quarter of India’s oil imports in December last year, analysts warn Delhi can ill afford to stop such purchases completely.
“India is unlikely to completely halt Russian oil imports, as the discounted crude remains vital to its energy security. However, the proposed US legislation could push India to significantly reduce its dependence,” Menon said.
Delhi would aim to diversify imports from the Middle East and the US, while seeking exemptions or even phased compliance, he said, adding that such a calibrated reduction would allow India to balance its energy needs with geopolitical risks.
India’s imports of Russian oil recorded a 29 per cent month-on-month decline in December, although there have been signs of a turnaround, according to an analysis by the Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air.
The drop was driven by a sharp reduction in imports by Reliance’s Jamnagar refinery, widely recognised as the world’s largest single-site oil refinery complex.
“India has already diversified [its oil imports], boosting US crude to 10-11 per cent of imports in late 2025 and ramping up from Saudi Arabia and Iraq,” said Srinivasan Balakrishnan, director of strategic engagements and partnerships at Indic Researchers Forum, a Delhi-based security and strategic affairs think tank.
Russia had been offering discounts of up to US$10 per barrel of crude oil, he said.
“A complete halt would inflate our import bill by US$6 billion to US$10 billion annually, risking inflation and growth in an 85 per cent import-dependent economy,” he said. “Geopolitically, Delhi won’t sacrifice strategic autonomy, we’ve navigated similar pressures before.”

Vivek Mishra, deputy director of strategic studies at the Observer Research Foundation, warned that trade between India and the US would grind to a halt if the steep tariffs were imposed.
“To my mind, 500 per cent tariffs is absurd. I think it is a tactic to compel India to come to the table and open up some of the sectors to the US which it has been resisting,” he said.
While the bill on buying Russian oil would seemingly target both China and India, Mishra noted that Beijing had far more leverage in trade negotiations because of assets such as rare earth minerals.
“India does not have the same cards and for Delhi it will be far more difficult,” he said.
The US is India’s largest export market, receiving about 18 per cent of its total goods exports, with a vast diaspora readily buying products shipped from their homeland.

India has been resisting US efforts to open up politically sensitive sectors such as farm and dairy products, which could invite backlash from farmers. At the same time, it has signalled readiness to lower import duties across a range of industries.
Balakrishnan said the tenor of ongoing negotiations suggested that most substantive differences had been largely resolved, underscoring that there were strategic compulsions on both sides to conclude a trade deal.
“For the US, India’s central role in the Indo-Pacific positions it as a critical counterweight to China, while India views the trade agreement as a pathway to enhanced market access and advanced technology,” he said.
Earlier this week, new US Ambassador to India Sergio Gor said Delhi would be asked to join the Pax Silica, a US-led initiative to counter China’s growing dominance in AI supply chains. The alliance already includes key Asia-Pacific allies – Japan, South Korea, Singapore and Australia – as well as Israel.

data.title: Hong Kong bar trends in 2026: comfort, classics and mindful drinking
data.description:
Hong Kong’s drinks scene is one of the world’s best – but also one of the world’s most diverse and volatile. Every year, the city has had to navigate – to varying degrees of success – trends and shifts in what bargoers enjoy, resulting in celebrated openings and sombre closures alike. Speakeasies (The Old Man, Please Don’t Tell, Room 309, Stockton) in the 2010s gave way to neighbourhood bars in the 2020s. Publications like Time magazine, Fortune and Drinks International all note that Gen Z drinks less overall than their predecessors, swapping evening bar crawls for daytime coffee raves.
As we ease into 2026, we sat down with some of Hong Kong’s leading mixologists to explore the latest trends shaping the industry – showing how our bars are adapting to the way we drink and how that, in turn, is redefining our nights out.
Neighbourhood bars are still in, so keep it simple

“I think of neighbourhood bars as focusing on simplicity and character, with an interior design that isn’t too elaborate,” says Antonio Lai, co-owner of Tastings Group, whose bars include Quinary, The Opposites and Draft Land.
A significant portion of bars that opened in the last two to three years have embraced some blend of relaxed atmosphere, simple drinks and a focus on community – think Socio, which upcycles surplus and waste ingredients from other venues, or Sugar King with its standing-room-only approach to Cuban-inspired cocktails. Hong Kong’s biggest success on the world stage, 2025 World’s Best Bar, Bar Leone is founded on similar tenets: accessible drinks and a convivial, casual atmosphere.

“Neighbourhood bars, to me, are places to have a drink in comfort,” says Samuel Kwok, who co-founded The Opposites with Lai. “Some places, like hotels, give the impression that you have to dress up a bit, or act or sit in a certain way,” he adds. “Sugar King and Mius [founded by Shelley Tai] have an atmosphere where you can just go in and have a drink, and the staff won’t bother you too frequently.”
Timeless, refined and classic flavours are in

If keeping it simple is at the root of the neighbourhood bar movement, classics are just one of the ways to get started. “People have always been wowed by bold, boundary-pushing innovation,” Singular Concepts and Tell Camellia co-founder Gagan Gurung says, “and we love serving [that need], but there’s been a noticeable shift to where guests are craving comfort. They’re reaching for those everyday, go-to cocktails that feel familiar, timeless and satisfying.”

Many of Hong Kong’s bars have pared back some part of their service to this effect. Both Sugar King and Montana opened on the strength of their daiquiris, also known as the “bartender’s handshake” (the cocktail represents a test of skill in producing the perfect balance of rum, lime and sweetness). Likewise, Kwok cites Bar Leone’s selection of classically inspired drinks as key to its success: “They are cocktails you could probably make at home and understand easily. Younger, newer bartenders these days are increasingly interested in the classics – a sort of return to form.”
“Trends come and go,” Gurung continues, “but the truth is everyone needs their all-time favourite, whether it’s a negroni, a daiquiri or a perfectly balanced Old Fashioned. Innovation excites, but comfort keeps people coming back.”
Less is more – comfort and moderation are key

For Ajit Gurung (no relation to Gagan Gurung), co-founder of The Savory Project, a part of this comfort lies in the way drinks are presented – especially when it comes to bars like his, which continue to find success with bold and innovative flavours. “I think tasting menu-style cocktails will dominate a lot of markets,” he says. “Small portions of multiple courses for cocktails is a great way to showcase new ideas and flavours without overwhelming guests.”

Draft Land and Bar Oasis are two examples of bars boasting exceptional cocktails on tap, allowing guests to sample before they order. “We’ve already pushed the boundaries of food and cocktail innovation – the real differentiator is in how we make people feel,” Gagan Gurung says. “It’s about the entire guest experience.”
Guests are becoming more selective when it comes to those experiences, seeking out spots they trust for drinks they are comfortable with. “Refined drinking is the way forward,” says Ajit Gurung. “Guests are more health conscious and are drinking less. They are selective about where they drink, how much sugar is in their drink, what spirits are used. They are following the bartenders as each has a different style of experiences they provide.”

“The younger generation may prefer to drink quality over quantity, just sipping a couple of glasses at any one bar,” Lai says. “They may also bar hop or prefer a low- or no-ABV style of drinking, and may start earlier in the day with coffee. The earlier they start, the earlier they can sleep, and the less they’ll be affected the next day.”
“People today are far more health conscious,” Gagan Gurung notes. “Whether it’s about wellness, mindfulness or just opting for a more balanced lifestyle, drinking habits are clearly shifting.”
Local ingredients for well-travelled guests

If bargoers are to step outside their comfort zone, it may well be to try a local ingredient far from home. “Guests now are well travelled and constantly looking for new experiences,” Ajit Gurung says. “Bold, culinary-driven ideas are the perfect category of cocktail for guests with more sophisticated palates.”
He is referring mainly to the resurgence of savoury cocktails, but Lai and Kwok see the same spirit of exploration extending to greater interaction with China. For Lai, Chinese-produced wines and spirits, including non-alcoholic sparkling spirits, will be more ubiquitous. Kwok adds that more bartenders from mainland China will go overseas for guest shifts.

“The new generation of [Chinese] bartenders is getting better in terms of language and communication, which has always been the biggest barrier,” he says. “China also has a lot more to show the world in terms of techniques and ingredients.” Kwok points out that China’s eight great cuisines are distinct and ripe for discovery. “Yunnan has great teas. Some areas have great herbs, while others have great spices and chillies. Every time I go to China, I see things I’ve never seen before thanks to this diversity.”
The reverse is also increasingly true – over the last few years, Bar Leone, Coa and Penicillin have opened branches in Shanghai. Lai hopes to open a branch of Quinary on the mainland, but obstacles remain.
“A surprising barrier is toilets, especially in second- and third-tier cities – even Hongkongers don’t know how to navigate the situation,” Kwok says. “We went to Chengdu once and every toilet was a squat toilet. If you’re hosting guests in China, taking them to truly local places requires them to navigate those toilets.”
“If I open a bar in China,” Lai says emphatically, “I will require sitting toilets!”

data.title: Time to end bid-rigging in Hong Kong with criminalisation and tougher enforcement
data.description:
Recent events in Hong Kong, particularly the tragic blaze at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po last November, have focused society’s attention on reforms to the legal system, especially those concerning building management and maintenance.
Corruption and improper practices in the tendering process for large-scale building renovation or maintenance have also become a hot-button issue.
In response, the government has established an independent committee to uncover the causes of the fire and examine underlying structural problems that contributed to the tragedy.
Bid-rigging, corruption and irregularities in tenders for major renovation works in multi-storey buildings are thus among the key issues on the independent committee’s agenda.
Hong Kong has many ongoing building maintenance projects in properties aged over 30 years as required by the Mandatory Building Inspection Scheme (MBIS).
Unfortunately, the MBIS has become liable to abuse by unscrupulous owners’ corporations, project consultants and contractors who can collude and engage in bid-rigging when undertaking building maintenance projects.
It stands to reason that bid-rigging remains the key enforcement priority for the city’s Competition Commission.
For example, in September 2025, the commission raided premises linked to alleged bid-rigging in 25 building maintenance projects worth over HK$600 million (US$76.9 million), involving coordinated “cover bids”.
This practice includes the submission of bids that are not likely to meet tender requirements – for example, bids that are artificially high – in order to influence the procurer to select a certain contractor.
However, despite the commission’s efforts, many ongoing building projects are still prone to bid-rigging.
The continued prevalence of bid-rigging in this sector deserves attention because the practice not only burdens residents financially but also risks safety by incentivising contractors to use cheap materials that are substandard, posing a threat to life and property.
So what does Hong Kong law currently provide on bid-rigging? The practice is classified as “serious anticompetitive conduct” under the Competition Ordinance (Cap 619).
However, it is only a civil offence punishable by fines, without criminal sanctions or imprisonment. Moreover, the existing legislative framework under the ordinance imposes significant evidentiary hurdles for the Competition Commission to bring proceedings.
To start a claim, the commission has to prove the existence of a bid-rigging agreement. However, bid-rigging arrangements are often made under the table and without contractual documentation or express agreements. The ordinance attempts to remedy this by bestowing wide investigative powers on the commission.
To enhance the deterrent effects of Hong Kong law with respect to bid-rigging, such conduct could also be made a criminal offence.
In fact, the criminalisation of bid-rigging aligns with global practice. Other jurisdictions, including the United States, the United Kingdom, Germany, Australia, Japan and South Korea, have formulated legislation to criminalise bid-rigging and other cartel conduct, such as price fixing, that damage competition in the marketplace.

For example, in the United Kingdom, the Enterprise Act 2002 criminalises individuals involved in bid-rigging arrangements. Individuals, if found liable, can be imprisoned for up to five years, with no limit on fines.
The Competition and Consumer Act 2010 in Australia also criminalises corporations and individuals who make or give effect to contracts with provisions that enable bid-rigging.
With reference to these jurisdictions, Hong Kong could introduce a cartel offence that holds the undertakings (which can include any sort of legal entity), individuals of the undertakings such as the directors, and others such as members of the owners’ corporation of a building, liable for bid-rigging or other misconduct.
For an enhanced deterrent effect, individuals liable for cartel offences should be subject to imprisonment, together with significant fines. The punishment should be proportionate to the enormous profits that undertakings or procuring agents can reap from large bid-rigging schemes.
But public consultation and careful study of appropriate sanctions are essential for any attempt at criminalisation to be successful.
Bid-rigging in the building maintenance market continues to be committed at the expense of residents’ welfare. It is time for reform and stiffer enforcement to take place to protect the public from the ramifications of this unlawful practice.
Martin Hui SC is the current vice-chairman of the Hong Kong Bar Association, a former deputy director of public prosecutions in Hong Kong and a criminal litigator.
Legal Tales is a new weekly column by senior members of the Hong Kong Bar Association presenting their perspectives on current affairs.
